name: Derived Units
description: Units made by combining the 7 base units together

content.overview: Derived units are made by combining base units. Just like you make words by combining letters, you make new units by combining the 7 base units. {{viz:diagram-01}}
content.explanation:
So we have 7 base units. But physics needs lots of other units too. Speed, force, energy, power, pressure... How do you make these units?

You multiply and divide the base units! {{viz:diagram-02}}

**Speed** = distance ÷ time = meters ÷ seconds = **m/s** (meters per second)

**Force** = mass × acceleration = kilograms × (meters ÷ seconds²) = **kg·m/s²** (this is also called a **Newton** or **N**)

**Energy** = force × distance = kg·m²/s² = **Joule** or **J**

**Power** = energy ÷ time = kg·m²/s³ = **Watt** or **W**

See the pattern? You start with base units and combine them. {{viz:chart-01}}

Sometimes scientists give these combinations fancy names:
- 1 kg·m/s² = 1 **Newton** (N) - a unit of force
- 1 kg·m²/s² = 1 **Joule** (J) - a unit of energy
- 1 kg·m²/s³ = 1 **Watt** (W) - a unit of power

But underneath, they're all just combinations of the 7 base units. {{viz:anim-01}}

Why do this? Because it makes physics clearer. Instead of saying 'kilograms times meters per second squared,' you say 'newtons.' Shorter, clearer, easier to work with.
keyPoints: Derived units = combinations of base units
All derived units come from combining the 7 base units
content.whyItMatters: Most units you see in physics are derived units. Understanding how they're built helps you understand what they mean and how to use them correctly.
